expectations.text: Rewrites story 1 without leaking implementation details like 'template engine' or 'S3' into the narrative
expectations.evidence: Story 1's narrative reads: 'As a learner, I can download a PDF certificate once I've completed a course, so that I have a shareable record of my achievement.' Neither 'template engine' nor 'S3' appear in the narrative; they are mentioned only in the preamble/What Changed as things deliberately removed.

expectations.text: Flags story 2 as a Fake Story / Task / not a user story
expectations.evidence: Story 2 title explicitly reads '(flagged — not a user story)' and the body says: '⚠ **Fake Story** — the need is internal implementation work, not a learner-facing outcome. The "developer adds a button" framing is a task dressed as a story.'

expectations.text: Splits story 3 into multiple independent stories (download, share, print, email)
expectations.evidence: Explicitly splits into '3a Download completion certificate as PDF', '3b Share certificate to LinkedIn', '3c Print completion certificate', '3d Email certificate to HR' — four independent stories.

expectations.text: Uses 'I can' throughout; no 'I want' remaining
expectations.evidence: Every rewritten narrative uses 'I can' (e.g., 'I can download a PDF certificate', 'I can share my certificate to LinkedIn', 'I can print my certificate', 'I can email my certificate'). No 'I want' remains in any story.

expectations.text: Every rewritten story has an explicit Title
expectations.evidence: Every story starts with a bolded '**Title:**' line (e.g., '**Title:** Download completion certificate as PDF', '**Title:** Share certificate to LinkedIn').

expectations.text: Replaces generic 'user' in story 1 with a concrete role (e.g., 'learner')
expectations.evidence: Story 1 uses 'As a learner'. What Changed note: 'Role changed from "user" to "learner" — the more concrete Apprendere role.'

expectations.text: Every story's INVEST check lists each of the six principles (I, N, V, E, S, T) individually with its own verdict — no compact 'all criteria met' one-liner
expectations.evidence: All six stories (1, 2, 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d) list each of I/N/V/E/S/T individually with per-principle verdicts ('pass', 'fail', 'partial'). No 'all criteria met' one-liner appears.

expectations.text: Each rewritten story has an inline 'What Changed' block (or similar per-story change note) directly following its INVEST check — not a single trailing summary table covering all stories
expectations.evidence: Stories 1 and 2 each have their own '**What Changed:**' block. However, stories 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d share a single combined 'What Changed (applies to the whole 3a-3d split)' block placed after 3a only — 3b, 3c, and 3d have no per-story change note of their own. Strict reading of the assertion requires each rewritten story to have its own inline block.

expectations.text: Does not reference system components, pages, endpoints, or concepts absent from the input drafts (e.g., no invented 'course-completion page', 'completion dashboard', or other named surfaces the user didn't write)
expectations.evidence: References to 'completion page' are present in the input (draft 2 says 'the completion page'). The response uses 'completion page' not 'course-completion page' or 'completion dashboard'. The '/certificate' endpoint is quoted from the input. LinkedIn, print, email, HR are all from draft 3. No invented surfaces.

suggestions.assertion: Each rewritten story has an inline 'What Changed' block (or similar per-story change note) directly following its INVEST check — not a single trailing summary table covering all stories
suggestions.reason: The assertion is ambiguous when the response splits one draft into several daughter stories. The with_skill output provided a single 'What Changed (applies to the whole 3a-3d split)' block adjacent to the split, which is arguably the most information-dense way to explain the split — penalising it reads as rewarding boilerplate. Consider rewording to 'per logical rewrite unit' or allow a single shared block for a split set.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are generally discriminating; the only soft spot is the per-story What-Changed rule when a draft is split.
